text:
This article presents the transformation in higher education from a common good to a commodity, which is adjusted to global market. Globalization along with international policies such as the General Agreement on Trade in Services (GATS) caused the transformation in higher education systems. Under globalization higher education exchange and knowledge flowing have been maintained, however, higher education was treated as a product consisting of skills that consumers can learn at educational institutions and therefore citizenship and civil society has been neglected. Besides, the subjecting higher education to WTO rules is seen as a considerable risk in terms of globalisation.

Threats to the maintenance of national academic standards, degree mills and growing global inequalities are all major concerns. The author uncovers a new neocolonialism in which multinational Western companies which expand their activities world wide (especially in the business and IT fields) take the most profitable sectors, leaving the less lucrative, yet vital, sectors like the basic sciences and local culture to the local and often struggling higher education institutions in developing countries. This not only endangers the academic independence of developing countries but also focuses on the wrong objective: education as a commodity rather than as a social and cultural good. The paper argues that globalisation requires a more global approach to education but at the same time calls for adequate protection of education against the market.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
